news_summary: Kharkiv is rebuilding with hope for peace, while new research reveals chimpanzees and bonobos form complex, human-like social bonds.
news_summary_fr: Kharkiv se reconstruit dans l'espoir de la paix, tandis que de nouvelles recherches révèlent que les chimpanzés et les bonobos nouent des liens sociaux complexes, semblables à ceux des humains.
news_summary_es: Járkov se está reconstruyendo con la esperanza de la paz, mientras que nuevas investigaciones revelan que los chimpancés y los bonobos establecen vínculos sociales complejos, similares a los de los humanos.

articles.title: Under fire, Kharkiv is already building for a peaceful tomorrow
articles.summary: Every day in Kharkiv begins with uncertainty: air raid sirens interrupt sleep; missiles strike residential neighbourhoods, industrial sites, and roads. Anxious citizens rush into metro stations during bombardments and children study underground.
articles.content:
Yet amid the destruction, Ukraine’s second-largest city is doing something that may seem almost impossible during wartime: preparing for a better future.

“We need to rebuild regardless of the war,” says Ihor Terekhov, the city’s mayor, “because if there is no reconstruction there will be only ruins, and those who left will not return.”

Kharkiv City Council

Kharkiv, once a thriving centre of industry, science, education and culture near Ukraine’s northeastern border, has become one of the country’s most heavily damaged cities since Russia’s full-scale invasion began.

According to Mr. Terekhov, around 13,000 buildings have been damaged or destroyed, including approximately 10,000 residential buildings. About 160,000 people have lost their homes. “Every day there is shelling, and it is terrible,” he says. “Just staying alive is exhausting.”

Amid the daily attacks, urban planners, architects, engineers and international organisations are working alongside Ukrainian officials to imagine what the city could become after the war.

Waiting is not an option

At the centre of this effort is the UN4UkrainianCities initiative led by the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE), which supports the revitalisation and modernisation of Kharkiv and the southern city of Mykolaiv. The project aims not only to respond to emergency needs, but also to help cities rebuild in a smarter, greener and more resilient way.

© Kharkiv City Coumcil/Vasil Golosniy

For many outsiders, long-term urban planning during an ongoing war may seem premature. But for those working on the ground, waiting is not an option.

“The country is still under attack, and emergency work is still very much needed,” says Thâmara Fortes, an architect and programme manager for the project. “But recovery and reconstruction have to happen in parallel.”

The idea, she explains, is that today’s emergency repairs also support tomorrow’s development. “If you construct something now without thinking long-term, in ten years it may no longer serve the city,” she says.

“So, we are helping the cities think not only about the emergency, but about how those interventions fit into the future.”

A new master plan

That future is being shaped through a new master plan for Kharkiv focused on sustainable infrastructure, affordable housing, innovative public spaces and economic renewal. The project combines strategic planning with pilot projects that test ideas in real neighbourhoods.

One of the most ambitious efforts focuses on North Saltivka, a heavily damaged residential district.

What began as an architectural competition has now moved into the technical implementation stage. Five residential blocks and a kindergarten are being redesigned with energy-efficient insulation, modular expansions and structural reinforcements.

The goal is practical: create detailed technical documentation that city authorities can immediately present to donors, investors or development banks when funding becomes available.

“We are moving from concepts to implementation,” Ms. Fortes says. “Not only analysing and assessing but also giving cities something they can actually build.”

Bold initiatives

The broader vision for Kharkiv includes five major pilot initiatives blending preservation, sustainability and innovation.

One project focuses on restoring the war-damaged historical centre while adapting old interiors for modern civic and cultural use. Another aims to transform industrial riverbanks into green public corridors along the city’s 25-kilometre riverfront system.

A science and technology district near major universities is intended to help retain young talent and diversify the economy, and industrial zones dependent on coal are being reimagined as clean manufacturing hubs powered by renewable energy.

The effort also extends beyond infrastructure. UNECE and Ukrainian partners are working on housing policy reforms at the national level, including legislation on social rental housing and housing stock management.

© Kharkiv City Council/Vasil Golosniy

‘Constantly under threat’

Meanwhile, local officials continue dealing with the brutal realities of war. “It is impossible to get used to this,” Mr. Terekhov says quietly. “People are constantly under threat.”

Still, he believes that now is the time to think about how to restore the city and imagine the future. “People live today with the hope that we will rebuild everything.”

That hope is especially important in a city known for its architectural heritage. Kharkiv’s museums, libraries and historic mansions have all suffered damage. Mr. Terekhov recalls strikes on the famed Korolenko State Scientific Library and historic buildings near the city’s art museum.

“These are pearls of Ukraine,” he says. “Some buildings cannot be restored exactly as they were because they were hit by several ballistic missiles. But we are trying to preserve this architecture.”

The reconstruction effort itself has evolved over the course of the war. According to Ms. Fortes, one of the key lessons has been flexibility. “Sometimes there is no electricity. Sometimes no internet. Sometimes people are in underground shelters,” she says. “So, we learned that the project needs to constantly adapt to the realities of the cities.”

‘Cities need to be prepared’

Ukraine is forcing architects and planners to rethink the very design of urban space. New housing projects in Kharkiv now incorporate dual-use underground areas that can function as everyday public infrastructure in peacetime and become shelters during attacks.

“In peacetime, it can be a parking lot. In wartime, it becomes a shelter,” Ms. Fortes explains. The same thinking is being applied to kindergartens and schools, some of which are being designed with underground learning spaces.

Fortes says the approach draws partly on lessons from Finland, where many cities have long integrated civil defence infrastructure into daily urban life. Underground facilities used for sports, parking or recreation during normal times can quickly transform into emergency shelters if needed.

She believes such ideas may become increasingly relevant far beyond Ukraine as cities worldwide confront not only war, but also climate disasters and other crises requiring greater urban resilience. “The crisis happens at the local level – on your street, in your house,” she says. “Cities need to be prepared.”

New vision dawns

In Kharkiv, preparedness now means more than shelters and emergency repairs. It means preserving communities, protecting identity and creating reasons for people to stay – or someday return.

“Our cities will no longer be the same as before the war,” Mr. Terekhov says. “We need a new vision.”

These interviews were conducted on the sidelines of the 2026 World Urban Forum in Baku, Azerbaijan, held between 17 and 22 May.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and inspiring effort to rebuild and modernize Kharkiv during an ongoing war, focusing on sustainable urban planning, resilience, and community preservation. This has broad societal impact by aiming to restore a heavily damaged city, improve living conditions, and prepare for future crises, supported by international cooperation and innovative approaches. The story is detailed and focused on a single topic of reconstruction and urban renewal amid conflict.
articles.personality_title: Kharkiv plans a safer, greener future despite ongoing war
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Kharkiv, Ukraine’s second-largest city, has been heavily damaged by missile attacks since Russia’s invasion began. Thousands of homes and buildings are destroyed, and many people have lost their houses. Despite the daily danger and destruction, city leaders are already planning how to rebuild.

**What happened** – The city’s mayor, Ihor Terekhov, and international groups like the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) are working together on a new master plan for Kharkiv. This plan focuses on rebuilding homes, schools, and public spaces in a smarter and greener way. They are redesigning neighborhoods with energy-saving features and making some areas safer by including underground shelters that can be used during attacks. Several pilot projects are underway, such as restoring historic buildings, creating green riverfront parks, and developing new science and technology areas.

**Impact** – This rebuilding effort is unusual because it is happening while the war continues. Instead of waiting for peace, planners are combining emergency repairs with long-term ideas to make the city stronger and more modern. The work also includes new housing policies and creative designs that help protect people during attacks. This approach not only helps Kharkiv but also shows how cities can prepare for future crises like wars or natural disasters.

**What's next step** – The city will continue moving from planning to actual building. Technical details are being prepared so that when funding is available from donors or banks, construction can start quickly. The pilot projects will test new ideas in real neighborhoods, and lessons learned will guide rebuilding across the city. Officials hope these efforts will encourage people to stay or return to Kharkiv after the war.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Even amid war, Kharkiv is rebuilding with smart, sustainable plans that protect its people and prepare the city for a safer future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Kharkiv prépare un avenir plus sûr et plus vert malgré la guerre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Kharkiv, la deuxième plus grande ville d’Ukraine, a été fortement endommagée par des attaques de missiles depuis le début de l’invasion russe. Des milliers de maisons et de bâtiments ont été détruits, et de nombreuses personnes ont perdu leur logement. Malgré le danger quotidien, les dirigeants de la ville planifient déjà sa reconstruction.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le maire Ihor Terekhov et des groupes internationaux comme la Commission économique des Nations Unies pour l’Europe (CEE-ONU) travaillent ensemble sur un nouveau plan directeur pour Kharkiv. Ce plan vise à reconstruire les maisons, écoles et espaces publics de manière plus intelligente et écologique. Ils repensent des quartiers avec des caractéristiques économes en énergie et intègrent des abris souterrains pour protéger la population pendant les attaques. Plusieurs projets pilotes sont en cours, comme la restauration de bâtiments historiques, la création de parcs verts le long des rivières, et le développement de zones scientifiques et technologiques.

**Impact** – Cet effort de reconstruction est inhabituel car il se fait en pleine guerre. Au lieu d’attendre la paix, les planificateurs combinent réparations d’urgence et idées à long terme pour rendre la ville plus forte et moderne. Ce travail comprend aussi de nouvelles politiques de logement et des designs innovants qui protègent les habitants. Cette approche aide non seulement Kharkiv, mais montre aussi comment les villes peuvent se préparer à d’autres crises comme les guerres ou les catastrophes naturelles.

**Prochaine étape** – La ville va continuer à passer de la planification à la construction. Les détails techniques sont préparés pour que, dès l’arrivée des financements, les travaux puissent commencer rapidement. Les projets pilotes testeront de nouvelles idées dans des quartiers réels, et les leçons tirées guideront la reconstruction de toute la ville. Les autorités espèrent que ces efforts encourageront les habitants à rester ou à revenir à Kharkiv après la guerre.

**Une phrase clé** – Malgré la guerre, Kharkiv se reconstruit avec des plans intelligents et durables qui protègent sa population et préparent la ville à un avenir plus sûr.
articles.personality_title_es: Járkov planifica un futuro más seguro y verde a pesar de la guerra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Járkov, la segunda ciudad más grande de Ucrania, ha sido gravemente dañada por ataques con misiles desde que comenzó la invasión rusa. Miles de casas y edificios han sido destruidos, y muchas personas han perdido sus hogares. A pesar del peligro diario, los líderes locales ya están planificando cómo reconstruir la ciudad.

**Qué pasó** – El alcalde Ihor Terekhov y grupos internacionales como la Comisión Económica para Europa de las Naciones Unidas (CEPE-ONU) están trabajando juntos en un nuevo plan maestro para Járkov. Este plan se centra en reconstruir viviendas, escuelas y espacios públicos de manera más inteligente y ecológica. Están rediseñando vecindarios con características que ahorran energía e incluyen refugios subterráneos para proteger a la gente durante los ataques. Hay varios proyectos piloto en marcha, como restaurar edificios históricos, crear parques verdes a lo largo de los ríos y desarrollar nuevas zonas de ciencia y tecnología.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo de reconstrucción es especial porque ocurre mientras la guerra continúa. En lugar de esperar la paz, los planificadores combinan reparaciones de emergencia con ideas a largo plazo para hacer la ciudad más fuerte y moderna. El trabajo también incluye nuevas políticas de vivienda y diseños creativos que protegen a las personas. Este enfoque no solo ayuda a Járkov, sino que muestra cómo las ciudades pueden prepararse para futuras crisis como guerras o desastres naturales.

**Próximo paso** – La ciudad seguirá avanzando de la planificación a la construcción. Se están preparando detalles técnicos para que, cuando haya fondos disponibles, la construcción pueda comenzar rápidamente. Los proyectos piloto probarán nuevas ideas en vecindarios reales, y las lecciones aprendidas guiarán la reconstrucción en toda la ciudad. Las autoridades esperan que estos esfuerzos animen a la gente a quedarse o regresar a Járkov después de la guerra.

**Una frase para recordar** – Incluso en medio de la guerra, Járkov se está reconstruyendo con planes inteligentes y sostenibles que protegen a su gente y preparan la ciudad para un futuro más seguro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a resilient cityscape showing partially ruined but steadily rebuilding apartment blocks with scaffolding and green plants growing around them, alongside a winding river bordered by newly planted trees and modern pedestrian paths, while abstract silhouettes of architects and planners holding blueprints are symbolized by glowing, translucent geometric shapes hovering above the city, representing hope, sustainable reconstruction, and a peaceful future emerging from the shadows of war.

articles.title: Chimpanzees and bonobos have human-like friend circles, study finds
articles.summary: Great apes appear to build friendships much like humans do. By studying grooming behavior, researchers discovered that chimpanzees and bonobos form close inner circles along with wider networks of weaker social connections. Chimpanzees focus on a few trusted partners and become more selective with age, while bonobos maintain a more egalitarian social style.
articles.content:
Humans often organize their relationships into layers, spending the most time with a small group of close friends and family while maintaining weaker connections with a larger number of acquaintances. A new international study suggests that this pattern is not unique to people.

Researchers from Utrecht University and Universidad Carlos III de Madrid found that chimpanzees and bonobos, our closest living relatives, also form social networks that resemble human friendship circles. The findings provide new insight into how complex social relationships may have evolved.

Great Apes Build Human-Like Social Networks

To investigate how great apes manage their social lives, researchers examined social grooming behavior in 24 groups of chimpanzees and bonobos. Grooming is one of the most important social activities among apes, helping to strengthen bonds and maintain relationships.

Using a mathematical model, the team analyzed how individuals distributed their limited time and social effort among other members of their group.

The results revealed a familiar pattern. Most apes devoted a large share of their grooming time to a small number of preferred partners while maintaining less intensive relationships with many others. This layered structure closely resembles the way human social circles are organized.

The study also found that apes living in larger groups tended to be more selective about where they invested their social attention, a trend that has also been observed in human social networks.

Chimpanzees and Bonobos Take Different Approaches

Although both species showed human-like social structures, they did not manage their relationships in exactly the same way.

Bonobos distributed their grooming time more evenly among group members, creating a more egalitarian social network. Chimpanzees, by contrast, concentrated more of their effort on a smaller number of favored companions.

These differences reflect broader distinctions in the social behavior of the two species.

Aging Changes Chimpanzee Friendships

People often become more selective about their relationships as they grow older, focusing more attention on a smaller inner circle of close connections. The researchers found a similar pattern among chimpanzees.

As chimpanzees age, they increasingly invest in fewer social partners. Bonobos, however, did not show the same narrowing of their social circles over time.

"Possibly, this is due to their more egalitarian social systems. Bonobos appear to live together in more fluid relationships, with social bonds that transcend group boundaries, something we rarely see in chimpanzees," explains Van Leeuwen.

Clues to the Evolution of Friendship

According to lead author Edwin van Leeuwen, the results suggest that similar principles govern how social relationships are formed and maintained across multiple species.

"Our findings suggest that the fundamental rules that guide how individuals allocate social effort apply across multiple species," says Van Leeuwen. "This reveals deep evolutionary continuity in how complex societies are organized."

At the same time, the differences between chimpanzees and bonobos indicate that there is more than one evolutionary strategy for managing social connections.

Van Leeuwen notes that understanding these patterns could improve scientists' understanding of cooperation, social learning, and emotional well-being in both humans and other animals.

"Understanding these patterns may reveal crucial insights for studying cooperation, social learning, and emotional well-being in both humans and other animals."
articles.reasoning: The study reveals meaningful scientific insights into the evolution of social behavior by showing that chimpanzees and bonobos form human-like friendship circles, which has broad implications for understanding social relationships, cooperation, and emotional well-being across species. This represents a significant advancement in knowledge about social evolution with potential benefits for science and society.
articles.personality_title: Chimpanzees and bonobos form friendship circles like humans, study shows
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Humans often organize their friendships in layers, with a small group of close friends and many weaker acquaintances. Scientists wondered if this way of socializing is unique to people or if other animals do it too.

**What happened** – Researchers from Utrecht University and Universidad Carlos III de Madrid studied grooming behavior in 24 groups of chimpanzees and bonobos. Grooming helps apes build social bonds. They found that both species spend most of their time grooming a few close friends while still keeping weaker ties with others. Chimpanzees focus more on a small number of trusted friends, especially as they get older, while bonobos spread their attention more evenly across the group.

**Impact** – This study shows that great apes, our closest relatives, manage friendships in ways very similar to humans. It reveals that the way social circles are formed is not just a human trait but has deep roots in evolution. Also, the differences between chimpanzees and bonobos suggest there are different ways to build social networks in nature.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use these findings to better understand how cooperation, learning, and emotional health develop in both humans and animals. Further research may explore how these social patterns affect group life and survival.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Chimpanzees and bonobos create friendship circles much like humans, showing that complex social networks have deep evolutionary origins.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les chimpanzés et les bonobos forment des cercles d’amis comme les humains, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les humains organisent souvent leurs amitiés en plusieurs couches, avec un petit groupe d’amis proches et de nombreux contacts plus faibles. Les scientifiques se sont demandé si cette façon de socialiser était unique aux humains ou si d’autres animaux la pratiquaient aussi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Utrecht et de l’Université Carlos III de Madrid ont étudié le comportement de toilettage chez 24 groupes de chimpanzés et bonobos. Le toilettage aide les singes à renforcer leurs liens sociaux. Ils ont découvert que les deux espèces passent la plupart de leur temps à toiletter quelques amis proches tout en maintenant des liens plus faibles avec d’autres. Les chimpanzés se concentrent davantage sur un petit nombre d’amis de confiance, surtout en vieillissant, tandis que les bonobos répartissent plus équitablement leur attention.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que les grands singes, nos plus proches parents, gèrent leurs amitiés de manière très similaire aux humains. Elle révèle que la formation des cercles sociaux n’est pas seulement un trait humain, mais a des racines profondes dans l’évolution. De plus, les différences entre chimpanzés et bonobos suggèrent qu’il existe plusieurs façons de construire des réseaux sociaux dans la nature.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser ces résultats pour mieux comprendre comment la coopération, l’apprentissage et la santé émotionnelle se développent chez les humains et les animaux. D’autres recherches pourraient explorer comment ces schémas sociaux influencent la vie de groupe et la survie.

**Une phrase clé** – Les chimpanzés et les bonobos créent des cercles d’amis comme les humains, montrant que les réseaux sociaux complexes ont des origines évolutives profondes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los chimpancés y bonobos forman círculos de amistad como los humanos, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los humanos suelen organizar sus amistades en capas, con un pequeño grupo de amigos cercanos y muchos conocidos con conexiones más débiles. Los científicos se preguntaron si esta forma de socializar es única de las personas o si otros animales también la practican.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Utrecht y la Universidad Carlos III de Madrid estudiaron el comportamiento de acicalamiento en 24 grupos de chimpancés y bonobos. El acicalamiento ayuda a los simios a fortalecer sus lazos sociales. Descubrieron que ambas especies dedican la mayor parte de su tiempo a acicalar a unos pocos amigos cercanos mientras mantienen vínculos más débiles con otros. Los chimpancés se enfocan más en un pequeño número de amigos de confianza, especialmente al envejecer, mientras que los bonobos distribuyen su atención de forma más equitativa.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que los grandes simios, nuestros parientes más cercanos, manejan sus amistades de manera muy parecida a los humanos. Revela que la formación de círculos sociales no es solo un rasgo humano, sino que tiene raíces profundas en la evolución. Además, las diferencias entre chimpancés y bonobos sugieren que existen distintas formas de construir redes sociales en la naturaleza.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar estos hallazgos para entender mejor cómo se desarrollan la cooperación, el aprendizaje y el bienestar emocional en humanos y animales. Investigaciones futuras podrían explorar cómo estos patrones sociales afectan la vida en grupo y la supervivencia.

**Una frase clave** – Los chimpancés y bonobos crean círculos de amistad como los humanos, mostrando que las redes sociales complejas tienen orígenes evolutivos profundos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two groups of great apes—chimpanzees and bonobos—grooming each other in distinct social circles: the chimpanzees clustered tightly in small, focused groups showing close bonds, while the bonobos are connected more evenly in larger, harmonious circles, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle light.
